summary: Monster He's Gone & The Other One with tight first set
blurb: The show delivers a powerhouse first set highlighted by Truckin' and China Cat Sunflower > I Know You Rider, then launches into a second‑set marathon of He’s Gone, The Other One, Me & Bobby McGee and a soaring close. Jerry’s lead work is on fire, Phil’s bass solos explode, and the band stays locked in from start to finish.
review: September 28, 1972 at the Stanley Theatre stands out as one of the band’s most electrifying late‑summer outings. The first set opens with a high‑energy Truckin' that rolls into a flawless sequence of Black Throated Wind and a classic China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider transition, setting a tight, upbeat tone for the night. The second half is where the magic truly erupts: He’s Gone launches an explosive Phil Lesh solo described as “sheets of sound,” which feeds into an extended The Other One that reviewers call a “monster” and “soaring.” From there the band weaves through Me & Bobby McGee, another pass at The Other One, Wharf Rat, Sugar Magnolia and a double Not Fade Away finish, all delivered with Jerry Garcia’s guitar on “tiger stuff,” immaculate tone, and a rhythm section that never lets up. Keith’s keyboards sit low but add just enough texture, while Bill Kreutzmann drives the jams with “funky” precision. Across every soundboard source the performance is consistently praised as one of the strongest of the 1972 tour, making this show a must‑listen for any Deadhead.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All twelve recordings (except one poor‑quality audience tape) describe the show in glowing terms—monster He’s Gone, soaring The Other One, flawless jams and consistently praised band interplay. No recording mentions systematic performance flaws; the only negative notes are technical (missing vocal on Loser, early SBD glitches). This uniform positive sentiment warrants a strong +1.6 adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Miller soundboard capture is crystal‑clear, balances all instruments, and has only minor issues (missing vocal on Loser). It presents the full show with excellent fidelity, making it the definitive listening source.

key_highlights: He’s Gone bass solo described as "sheets of sound"
"The Other One" marathon praised as a monster jam
Crystal‑clear Miller SBD capture delivers pristine audio
First‑set China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider transition shines

band_performance.Jerry: Lead work is on fire—"tiger stuff" on He’s Gone, immaculate Strat tone, mean and triumphant solos on The Other One; consistently praised as a major reason to love this show.
band_performance.Phil: Bass shines with "sheets of sound" solo on He’s Gone, tight zone runs, jazzy interplay, and a rolling bomb energy that fuels the long jams.
band_performance.Bob: Rhythm guitar is creative and dominant—UniVibe textures stand out on Me & Bobby McGee; often high in the mix but adds beautiful groove throughout.
band_performance.Billy: Drumming described as excellent, perfect, and funky; drives the extended jams with precision and power.
